context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this whole problem set, honestly. Could you just explain from the beginning what’s going on, especially with the normal modes part and maybe the optics too?
exchanges.tutor:
Let's take this one step at a time — that's the best way to tackle a problem set like this.

Let's start with Problem 8.1 on the 2D beaded string. Before I say anything, tell me: what do you already know about normal modes from your course so far? What's the basic idea behind them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of everything, which would amount to doing the work for them. Instead, I should focus them on one problem at a time and use Socratic questioning to get them to activate their prior knowledge. I'll start with Problem 8.1 since they mentioned normal modes first, and ask them what they already know to gauge where to begin.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, just that they’re some kind of special vibration patterns. But I don’t really remember how to set them up here, and the 2D grid is making it feel way more confusing.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. You said they're 'special vibration patterns' — what makes them special? Specifically, what are all the beads doing in a normal mode that they wouldn't necessarily do in a random vibration?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague intuition about normal modes but hasn't articulated the key property that makes them 'special.' I should draw out that understanding through a targeted question rather than explaining it myself. I'll focus on what makes a normal mode special - that all parts oscillate at the same frequency - and connect it to what they already know about 1D systems before tackling the 2D complexity.

exchanges.student: I think they all oscillate with the same frequency maybe? But then I get stuck because I don’t know if that means same amplitude too, or same phase, or if every bead can be different. I’m kind of confused about all of that.
exchanges.tutor: You're right about the frequency — that's the key property. Now think about this: in the examples given in the problem, some beads have '+', some have '−', and some have '0'. What do you think those signs are telling you about how the beads relate to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the key property (same frequency) but is confused about amplitude and phase. I should affirm the correct part and guide them to think about phase specifically - in a normal mode, beads can have different amplitudes but phases are either 0 or π (in phase or out of phase). Rather than explaining this, I'll ask a targeted question to help them reason it out.

exchanges.student: I guess + and − mean opposite directions, and 0 means that bead doesn’t move in that mode. But I still feel like I’m missing the bigger picture and how you’d get all nine of them from scratch. Could you maybe just walk through the whole setup more generally first?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the signs. Now here's a key question to get the bigger picture: you've studied normal modes on a 1D beaded string before. For a 1D string with 3 beads, how many normal modes are there, and roughly what do they look like? Once you answer that, think about whether there's a connection to the 2D case.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly interpreted the +/-/0 signs, which is good progress. However, they're asking me to walk through the whole setup, which would be giving too much away. Instead, I should affirm their interpretation and nudge them toward thinking about how 2D normal modes can be built from 1D normal modes - a key insight for this problem. This is a guided discovery approach that keeps them doing the thinking.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t remember the 1D case very well either. I vaguely remember sine patterns maybe, but I can’t tell how that turns into this 3x3 grid, and now I’m also mixing it up with the optics problems. Could you just give me the broad idea from the start?
exchanges.tutor:
Let's stay focused — we're working on Problem 8.1 only for now, and mixing in optics won't help either of us.

Let's go back to basics. Forget memorizing patterns. For a single bead of mass m attached to a string under tension T, what force does the string exert on the bead if it's displaced? Can you write Newton's second law for it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting overwhelmed and trying to get me to explain everything broadly, which would essentially be giving away the solution. They're also mixing up problems. I need to set clear boundaries, keep them focused on one problem, and use a simpler question to help them recall the 1D case from first principles rather than memory. I'll ask them to think about Newton's second law for a single bead on a string, which is the true starting point.

exchanges.student: I’m not confident on that either. I think it’s something like restoring force from neighboring beads, but I’m fuzzy on the small-displacement approximation and which terms come from horizontal vs vertical strings. I feel like I need the overall setup more than one tiny piece.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but walking through the whole setup is exactly what I can't do — that's your job to work through.

Here's a concrete foothold: for a bead displaced by a small amount z in the transverse direction, the restoring force from one string segment of tension T and length a is approximately Tz/a. With that, can you write the equation of motion for bead (j,k) in the 3×3 grid, accounting for its four neighbors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for the big picture instead of engaging with specific questions. This is a pattern of avoidance rather than genuine confusion. I need to be firm about my role and redirect them to engage with the foundational question. However, I can give them a small concrete hint about the restoring force to get them unstuck, without giving away the setup. I should also be direct that I won't walk through the whole thing for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe it’s something with the differences between a bead and its neighbors in both directions, like a discrete Laplacian? But I’m not sure about the coefficients or signs, and I’m still pretty lost on how that turns into the actual mode shapes.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really good insight — yes, it has that discrete Laplacian structure. Now commit it to paper: using the restoring force I described, write out the equation of motion for bead (j,k). What are the four neighbors contributing, and what are the coefficients in front of each term?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine conceptual leap by recognizing the discrete Laplacian structure - this is a significant insight that deserves acknowledgment. I should affirm this and now push them to write it out explicitly. They're close to being able to set up the equation of motion, which is the critical first step. I should keep pushing them to commit something to paper rather than staying vague.

exchanges.student:
I think it should be something like  
\(m \ddot z_{j,k} =\) stuff from left/right with \(T_h\) plus stuff from up/down with \(T_v\), but I don’t trust myself on the exact expression. I’m still not seeing the full path after that, so could you just explain the general method all at once?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — now stop saying 'stuff' and write the actual terms. Each neighbor contributes T*(z_neighbor - z_{j,k})/a. Can you write the full equation with all four neighbors explicitly? Once you have that, I'll ask you the next question.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - they have the right structure in mind. But they keep asking for the full explanation at once, which I must refuse. I should affirm what they have, push them to write the explicit expression rather than 'stuff', and then ask one targeted question about what to do next. I should also note the redundancy in their requests for full explanations.

exchanges.student: I’m still unsure and kind of overwhelmed by it. Could you just show me the full equation explicitly so I can at least see what I’m aiming for?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't show you the full equation — that's a firm boundary for me as your tutor.

But here's the thing: you already have everything you need. You said it yourself — Th terms from left/right neighbors, Tv terms from up/down neighbors, and I told you each term looks like T*(z_neighbor − z_{j,k})/a. Just write it out. An imperfect attempt is fine — that's how learning works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me directly to give the answer, which I must refuse. They've been going in circles and expressing overwhelm. This is the third or fourth time they've asked for a full explanation or answer directly. I should be firm but encouraging, and remind them that they actually have enough to write the equation themselves based on what they've already said and what I've told them. I should be direct and push them to just try writing it, even if imperfect.

exchanges.student: I get that, I’m just really stuck and it feels like I’m piecing together random fragments. Could you at least give me the broad structure of the solution after the equation, like what assumption I make for the mode shape and why it works?
exchanges.tutor:
We've been going in circles, and I think it's time to break that pattern.

You have enough pieces: the equation structure, the four-neighbor restoring forces, the +/−/0 sign interpretation. Before we go any further, write me your best attempt at the equation of motion for bead (j,k) — even if it's messy or wrong. That's the only way to move forward productively.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been asking variations of the same question repeatedly - asking for the full picture, the full equation, the full method. This is now clearly a redundant pattern across many exchanges. I need to be firm, redirect them, and ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt rather than continuing this cycle. I should also note that they do have enough to make a start.
